stem: A retail study measures customer satisfaction score across 5 chains, each containing multiple stores. chain A has 10 observations with mean 44.7 /10 (within-chain SD = 8.1). chain B has 8 observations with mean 73.0 /10 (within-chain SD = 6.4). chain C has 15 observations with mean 52.9 /10 (within-chain SD = 5.4). chain D has 8 observations with mean 50.0 /10 (within-chain SD = 5.7). chain E has 10 observations with mean 48.7 /10 (within-chain SD = 4.2). The between-chain standard deviation is uncertain, estimated between 6.2 and 15.5 /10. Using a hierarchical Bayesian model, estimate the true customer satisfaction score for chain C, accounting for shrinkage toward the grand mean and the uncertainty in the between-chain variation.

ground_truth.answer: Hierarchical estimate for chain C: 52.9 /10 (shrunk from raw 52.9). Range [52.9, 53.0] as between-chain SD varies from 6.2 to 15.5. Pooled mean: 53.2.

solution_steps: Compute pooled (grand) mean: 53.2 /10 (weighted average of 5 chain means).
Shrinkage factor for chain C: B = σ²_between / (σ²_between + σ²_within/15). At σ_between=10.3: B = 0.982.
Interval [52.9, 53.0] captures uncertainty from the unknown between-group variance.
common_failure_modes: Using the raw observed mean (52.9) without shrinkage
Using only the grand mean (53.2) and ignoring group-specific data
Not recognizing that small-sample groups should be shrunk more toward the grand mean
Treating the between-group variance as known rather than uncertain
